name: Residential Operations & Environment Strategy
consequences: Immediate: Safety baseline preserved and domestic ambience maintained → Systemic: Throughput rises 25% via modular room use and dynamic staffing → Strategic: Enables scalable, privacy-conscious expansion and external collaborations; Trade-offs: Safety vs Naturalistic Behavior; Privacy vs Throughput
options: Preserve a naturalistic residential setting with standard safety features and fixed night staffing to maximize welfare and measurement fidelity.
Enable modular safety upgrades and flexible staffing to lift throughput while maintaining welfare and data quality.
Adopt a privacy-preserving, modular environment with edge-computing triage and external partnerships to scale to 12-16 suites within 18 months.
review: Trade-off: Controls Safety vs Naturalistic Behavior. Weakness: The options fail to consider long-term maintenance costs and staffing burnout.

name: Data Governance & Privacy Strategy
consequences: Immediate: Consent and de-identification controls tightened → Systemic: Cross-site data validation improves by 30% via controlled licensing for data sharing → Strategic: Enables broader benchmarking while preserving privacy; Trade-offs: Privacy vs Data Utility; Regulatory compliance vs speed
options: Maintain local data stewardship with strict de-identification and offline storage, limiting external access to sustain risk posture.
Adopt standardized governance with de-identified data exchange under formal data-use agreements to enable external validation without compromising privacy.
Establish a privacy-preserving analytics framework using federated processing across partner labs and a cross-institution data-sharing consortium with differential privacy and controlled licensing.
review: Trade-off: Privacy vs Data Utility. Weakness: The options fail to consider cross-border regulatory variability and a concrete licensing framework.

name: Adaptive Clinical-Research Protocol Strategy
consequences: Immediate: Interim analyses enable safe protocol adjustments → Systemic: Data yield per participant increases by 20% due to scheduled night-pattern adaptations → Strategic: Accelerates method validation and publication cadence; Trade-offs: Flexibility vs Reproducibility
options: Maintain a fixed pre-admission protocol with rigid screening and limited interim changes to protect data integrity.
Incorporate planned adaptive elements with milestone-based modifications to Night Schedule and observation windows under pre-specified criteria.
Implement a modular, data-driven protocol with real-time decision rules and cross-site validation, complemented by a consortium-based method optimization for shared learning.
review: Trade-off: Flexibility vs Reproducibility. Weakness: The options fail to define risk management for protocol changes and safety oversight during adaptive changes.

name: Participant Recruitment & Engagement Strategy
consequences: Immediate: Expanded outreach increases eligibility screening and consent rates → Systemic: Recruitment throughput improves by 40% via targeted channels and partner referrals → Strategic: Accelerates enrollment to meet sample targets while preserving representativeness; Trade-offs: Speed vs Representativeness
options: Rely on established channels (clinic referrals, local advertisements) with standard consent processes and fixed compensation.
Broaden recruitment through regional neurologists, patient groups, and enhanced compensation aligned with stay length to boost participation.
Create a regional participant consortium funded by sponsor-backed participation credits and hybrid remote pre-screening with on-site verification to rapidly scale enrollment.
review: Trade-off: Speed vs Representativeness. Weakness: The options fail to consider consent fatigue and equitable access across demographic groups.

name: Sensing Infrastructure & Analytics Architecture Strategy
consequences: Immediate: Current sensing stack remains compliant with privacy constraints → Systemic: Data richness and annotation throughput increase by 30% through higher fidelity nights and streamlined scoring → Strategic: Enables robust event-triage model validation and cross-lab reuse; Trade-offs: data richness vs privacy; cost vs quality
options: Maintain existing tiered sensing with standard data handling, ensuring privacy constraints and offline scoring.
Upgrade to higher fidelity nocturnal measurements with more participants, improved synchronization, and optimized annotation throughput.
Adopt a modular, privacy-preserving analytics stack with edge-enabled processing and federated triage scoring across partner sites, plus a cross-lab data-annotation consortium for licenses.
review: Trade-off: Data Richness vs Privacy. Weakness: The options fail to consider vendor lock-in and interoperability constraints within the host IT infrastructure.

name: Adaptive Facility and Governance Strategy
consequences: Immediate: Safety and privacy protections remain robust while enabling controlled testing of expansion; Systemic: 25% faster scaling through modular space design and standardized operating procedures, reducing onboarding time for new staff and sites; Strategic: enables rapid replication to additional cohorts or sites with higher upfront capital and tighter governance. Trade-offs: Safety/privacy controls slow expansion and add oversight complexity.
options: Maintain 8 active suites with enhanced safety and preserve domestic ambience, deferring major renovations and data-system upgrades until predefined safety and data-quality KPIs are achieved.
Expand to 12 modular suites with flexible staffing, deploy centralized data triage software, and implement milestone-based budget injections tied to enrollment and event capture KPIs.
Reconfigure into a modular, privacy-preserving, federated-analytics campus with cross-institution collaboration and outcome-based partnerships to fund expansion as KPIs are met.
review: Trade-off: Safety and Privacy vs. Operational Flexibility. Weakness: The options fail to anticipate regulatory delays or staff burnout that slow scaling.

name: Sensing and Data Architecture Strategy
consequences: Immediate: Upgrades to data flow and sensing capabilities improve capture fidelity; Systemic: 25% faster scaling through standardized data formats and reliable on-prem pipelines, enabling faster onboarding of new technicians; Strategic: unlocks cross-site analytics and richer phenotyping, but increases capital expenditure and privacy governance complexity. Trade-offs: data richness vs. privacy/cost.
options: Maintain current dry-electrode EEG and mattress sensors, on-prem storage, encrypted backups, and restrict data streams to essential channels; avoid cloud processing.
Introduce hybrid sensing with cloud-backed processing, standardize data formats (EEG-BIDS), implement event tagging pipeline, and moderate sampling rates.

Deploy a fully integrated sensing fabric with on-device feature extraction and privacy-preserving federated analytics; establish cross-institution de-identified data lake and a new funding model to support expansion.
review: Trade-off: Data depth vs. privacy and cloud-cost exposure. Weakness: Options insufficiently address cross-site data governance and vendor lock-in risks.

name: Recruitment and Study Flow Strategy
consequences: Immediate: Recruitment efficiency improves with broader reach; Systemic: 25% faster ramp-up of enrolled participants through diversified channels and optimized scheduling; Strategic: increases statistical power and generalizability, but raises regulatory scrutiny and variability in data quality. Trade-offs: speed vs. representativeness.
options: Maintain current recruitment channels, per-night compensation of €80, and keep stay cap; do not alter inclusion criteria.
Broaden recruitment channels to regional clinics; implement retention bonuses; restructure to enroll 50–70 participants; adjust inclusion criteria.
Decentralized registry with remote pre-screening and home-based intermittent monitoring; outcome-based reimbursement; cross-site expansion.
review: Trade-off: Recruitment speed vs. sample representativeness. Weakness: Options do not fully address potential misalignment between self-reported episodes and clinically validated events.

name: Data Governance, Ethics, and Publication Cadence Strategy
consequences: Immediate: Privacy protections remain stringent while enabling more timely data interpretation; Systemic: 25% faster scaling through streamlined approvals and reproducible pipelines, reducing lead times for publications; Strategic: enhances external credibility and reproducibility, but elevates governance overhead and potential limits on real-time collaboration. Trade-offs: Privacy protections vs openness.
options: Continue with current de-identification and restricted video constraints; publish after data capture.
Implement expanded de-identification pipelines, external data-use agreements, quarterly outputs, and faster peer review.
Establish an open data commons with cryptographic access controls and standardized, federated scoring protocol for external researchers.
review: Trade-off: Privacy protections vs openness. Weakness: Options underplay data curation and reproducibility across external researchers.

name: Partnerships and Sustainability Strategy
consequences: Immediate: Diversified funding reduces single-source risk; Systemic: 25% faster scaling through staged, milestone-based investments and shared resources, enabling broader study scope; Strategic: accelerates long-term impact and multi-site collaboration, but increases governance complexity and potential misalignment of incentives. Trade-offs: funding stability vs independence.
options: Pursue existing grants (DFG, University of Bonn) and internal funding; maintain current cost envelope; no pharma or external services.
Pursue additional grants and industry partnerships with defined rights; share publications; scale participant numbers.
Adopt a venture-like, milestone-based multi-funder funding model; offer external research services with outcome-based payments; foster cross-sector collaboration.
review: Trade-off: Funding stability vs independence. Weakness: Options may undervalue long-term sustainability beyond three years.

name: Residential Monitoring Architecture
consequences: Immediate: Deploy modular sensing with standardized interfaces → Systemic: 25% faster scaling through reusable data pipelines and cross-site replication → Strategic: improves scalability but increases upfront capital risk and governance complexity significantly.
options: Maintain eight-suite core and intensify on-site scoring with safety buffers, deferring expansion until data quality metrics meet predefined thresholds.
Scale to twelve–sixteen suites with automation-assisted annotation and real-time flagging, preserving two independent raters for adjudication.
Adopt edge-to-cloud federated analytics and a subscription-based triage service to enable remote collaboration and multi-site data sharing under privacy constraints.
review: Trade-off: Controls Speed vs. Data Quality. Weakness: The options fail to consider long-term patient privacy risk and regulatory complexity.

name: Data Governance and Privacy Strategy
consequences: Immediate: Centralize de-identification and enforce strict access controls; Systemic: 25% faster data curation through standardized privacy filters and audit trails; Strategic: reduces leakage risk but may constrain collaboration and replication.
options: Preserve strict de-identification and local storage with no beyond-institution data access.
Institute tiered data sharing with controlled access via data use agreements and a governance board.
Launch a data marketplace with centralized governance and de-identified datasets accessible under a trusted access framework.
review: Trade-off: Privacy Centralization vs. Collaboration. Weakness: The options fail to consider external regulatory audits and cross-institution governance challenges.

name: Academic-Industry Collaboration Model
consequences: Immediate: Align funding with scientific milestones and preserve publication independence; Systemic: 25% faster knowledge transfer via shared resources and synchronized reporting; Strategic: accelerates translation but risks diluted verification and control.
options: Maintain traditional research-only collaborations with milestone-based funding and standard publication rights.
Establish staged co-development with pharmaceutical partners on non-diagnostic metrics; shared authorship and milestone-revenue sharing.
Launch a cross-sector collaboration framework with co-ownership of results, joint governance, and licensing for downstream event-triage tools.
review: Trade-off: Publication Velocity vs. Independence. Weakness: The options fail to consider conflicts of interest and opaque governance risk.

name: Recruitment and Retention Strategy
consequences: Immediate: Expand outreach and provide travel support; Systemic: 25% improvement in enrollment efficiency via dynamic screening and retention protocols; Strategic: improves representativeness but increases logistical complexity and budget volatility significantly.
options: Maintain tight pre-screening with high per-night compensation and strict inclusion criteria, preserving safety and data quality.
Engage community partnerships, flexible scheduling, and robust retention protocols to broaden participation and minimize attrition.
Implement rolling enrollment with dynamic inclusion criteria, flexible travel support, and remote pre-screening to maximize reach while preserving safety.
review: Trade-off: Reach vs. Safety. Weakness: The options fail to consider regional participant burden and potential selection bias from travel requirements.

name: Technological Change and Staffing Model
consequences: Immediate: Deploy cross-training and remote dashboards to reduce on-site burden; Systemic: 25% reduction in night-shift fatigue via automated triage and remote oversight; Strategic: increases staffing flexibility but challenges continuity long-term.
options: Maintain the current staffing model with 12-hour night shifts, on-site technicians, and safety margins, keeping expansion contingent on pilot data.
Cross-train staff for multiple roles, invest in remote dashboards, and implement automated quality control to reduce manual workload.
Deploy autonomous sensor maintenance and machine-learning-assisted remote monitoring with a modular, contract-based staffing platform leveraging global experts.
review: Trade-off: Autonomy vs. Continuity. Weakness: The options fail to consider regulatory oversight and data integrity risks with autonomous operations.
